verdicts.new_stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with sudden palpitations for 50 minutes. She has had similar self-terminating episodes since her teens. She is haemodynamically stable. Her past history includes moderate persistent asthma managed with budesonide-formoterol; she had a hospital admission with status asthmaticus 3 years ago and reports wheeze with viral illnesses. She takes the combined oral contraceptive pill and drinks 2 to 3 coffees daily. On arrival she is anxious but well perfused. ECG shows a regular narrow-complex tachycardia at 184 bpm with no clearly visible P waves and a normal QRS axis. A modified Valsalva manoeuvre with leg elevation has been attempted twice without success.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: IV adenosine 6 mg rapid push followed by 20 mL saline flush with continuous ECG recording
new_options.rationale: Adenosine is normally first-line after failed vagal manoeuvres in stable narrow-complex SVT, but it can precipitate severe bronchospasm and is relatively contraindicated in patients with significant active asthma (eTG Cardiovascular; AMH).

new_options.text: IV verapamil 5 mg slowly over 2 minutes with continuous ECG and blood pressure monitoring
new_options.rationale: In stable narrow-complex SVT where adenosine is relatively contraindicated by active asthma, IV verapamil (or diltiazem) is the preferred alternative provided LV function is preserved and the rhythm is genuinely narrow-complex (eTG Cardiovascular; AMH).

new_options.text: Synchronised DC cardioversion at 50 joules under procedural sedation with continuous monitoring
new_options.rationale: Synchronised cardioversion is reserved for haemodynamically unstable SVT (hypotension, shock, ischaemia, severe heart failure); she remains stable so pharmacological termination is preferred (ANZCOR 11.10).

new_options.text: Oral metoprolol 25 mg with discharge home and cardiology outpatient follow-up if symptoms persist
new_options.rationale: Oral beta-blockade does not acutely terminate SVT and discharge in active tachyarrhythmia is unsafe; non-selective beta-blockade also risks bronchospasm in asthma (ANZCOR; eTG).

new_options.text: IV amiodarone 300 mg loading dose over 60 minutes via a peripheral large-bore cannula
new_options.rationale: Amiodarone is reserved for wide-complex tachycardia or refractory arrhythmias; it is not first-line for stable narrow-complex SVT and carries significant proarrhythmic and toxicity profile (ANZCOR; eTG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 31-year-old woman is reviewed by her GP for asthma. Over the past 3 months she has had daytime symptoms 3 to 4 times per week, nocturnal waking once weekly, and uses her salbutamol inhaler roughly 4 times per week. She has had no exacerbations requiring oral steroids in the past year. Her inhaler technique is excellent on observation. She is currently on salbutamol 100 microgram pMDI as needed only. She is planning pregnancy in the next 6 months and asks specifically about whether she can keep using her reliever as her only inhaler in pregnancy. FEV1 is 88 percent predicted with bronchodilator reversibility of 14 percent. She has no rhinitis, reflux, or known occupational trigger.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

new_options.text: Continue salbutamol PRN alone with a written asthma action plan and review after conception
new_options.rationale: Salbutamol-only therapy is no longer recommended at any step in adult asthma per the Australian Asthma Handbook (2024); her symptoms also meet criteria for poor control (SABA use greater than 2/week, nocturnal symptoms) and uncontrolled asthma in pregnancy carries higher fetal risk than ICS exposure.
source_refs: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)

new_options.text: Commence low-dose budesonide-formoterol 200/6 used as anti-inflammatory reliever only with written action plan
new_options.rationale: Australian Asthma Handbook Step 1-2 adult therapy is low-dose ICS-formoterol as anti-inflammatory reliever (AIR); budesonide is the ICS with the strongest pregnancy safety data and formoterol is acceptable, so AIR is preferred ahead of conception over SABA-only or daily ICS plus SABA.
source_refs: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)

new_options.text: Commence daily montelukast 10 mg nocte with continued salbutamol PRN as reliever
new_options.rationale: Leukotriene receptor antagonists are an add-on option in selected patients; they are inferior to ICS for first-line control and TGA has a boxed warning about neuropsychiatric effects, making montelukast an inappropriate first-line choice especially pre-conception.
source_refs: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)

new_options.text: Commence high-dose fluticasone-salmeterol 500/50 twice daily with salbutamol PRN
new_options.rationale: High-dose ICS-LABA over-treats mild-persistent asthma; stepwise escalation begins with low-dose AIR therapy (Australian Asthma Handbook).
source_refs: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)

new_options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 25 mg daily for 2 weeks then taper with continued salbutamol PRN
new_options.rationale: Oral corticosteroids are reserved for acute exacerbations or severe poorly controlled asthma; chronic oral steroids are inappropriate when inhaled options have not been trialled (Australian Asthma Handbook; AMH).
source_refs: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)

verdicts.new_stem: An 82-year-old nursing home resident with advanced Parkinson disease and known dysphagia is brought to ED with 24 hours of fever, productive cough, and confusion. The nursing home reports she was found coughing during dinner the previous evening. She was hospitalised 3 weeks ago for a urinary tract infection and received IV ceftriaxone and oral ciprofloxacin during that admission. She has no documented multi-drug-resistant organism. On examination she is febrile and tachypnoeic with right lower zone crackles. CXR shows right lower lobe consolidation. Bloods show neutrophilic leukocytosis and creatinine 96 micromol/L. She has no penicillin allergy.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate empirical antibiotic therapy?

new_options.text: IV amoxicillin-clavulanate 1.2 g eight-hourly empirically without antipseudomonal cover
new_options.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is the standard empirical choice for community-onset aspiration pneumonia without risk factors, but her recent hospitalisation and broad-spectrum antibiotic exposure within 90 days place her in the higher-risk category requiring broader empirical cover (eTG Respiratory).

new_options.text: IV piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g six-hourly empirically with daily microbiology review
new_options.rationale: Recent hospitalisation plus recent broad-spectrum antibiotic exposure are eTG-listed risk factors for Gram-negatives including Pseudomonas in aspiration pneumonia; broader empirical cover with piperacillin-tazobactam is recommended pending cultures, with de-escalation once organism known (eTG Respiratory; Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic).

new_options.text: Oral amoxicillin 1 g three times daily as monotherapy without anaerobic cover
new_options.rationale: Amoxicillin alone lacks beta-lactamase coverage for oral anaerobes and Gram-negatives, and oral therapy is inappropriate in this severity with hypoxia and confusion (eTG Respiratory).

new_options.text: IV ceftriaxone 1 g daily plus IV metronidazole 500 mg twelve-hourly empirically
new_options.rationale: Ceftriaxone-metronidazole does not cover Pseudomonas and is less appropriate given her recent ceftriaxone exposure; routine metronidazole is no longer recommended by eTG even when amoxicillin-clavulanate is used (Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic).

new_options.text: IV vancomycin 1.5 g twelve-hourly plus IV ciprofloxacin 400 mg twelve-hourly empirically
new_options.rationale: Vancomycin plus ciprofloxacin lacks adequate anaerobic cover and empirical vancomycin is not indicated without MRSA risk factors; recent fluoroquinolone exposure also makes empirical ciprofloxacin a poor choice (eTG Respiratory).

verdicts.new_stem: A 58-year-old man presents to ED with palpitations and mild dyspnoea that began approximately 32 hours ago, but he reports a similar but milder episode 4 days ago that he ignored. He has hypertension on perindopril and no other medical history. He is haemodynamically stable. ECG confirms atrial fibrillation at 102 bpm. Bedside transthoracic echocardiogram shows preserved LV function (LVEF 60 percent), mild left atrial dilatation (LA 42 mm), and no significant valvular disease. He has no prior stroke, peripheral vascular disease, diabetes, or heart failure, and has never been anticoagulated. CHA2DS2-VA score is 1 (hypertension). The team would like to attempt rhythm control on this presentation.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate approach to cardioversion?

new_options.text: Proceed to electrical cardioversion now without anticoagulation since current episode is under 48 hours
new_options.rationale: The 48-hour window applies only when AF onset is genuinely certain; his earlier symptomatic episode 4 days ago makes total duration uncertain, mandating either 3 weeks of therapeutic anticoagulation or a TOE before cardioversion (CSANZ/NHFA AF 2018).

new_options.text: Commence therapeutic anticoagulation and perform TOE-guided cardioversion, then continue anticoagulation for at least 4 weeks regardless of CHA2DS2-VA
new_options.rationale: When AF onset is uncertain or greater than 48 hours, CSANZ requires either 3 weeks of therapeutic anticoagulation or TOE-guided cardioversion; anticoagulation must continue for at least 4 weeks post-cardioversion due to atrial stunning, independent of CHA2DS2-VA score, which only governs longer-term anticoagulation.

new_options.text: Commence aspirin 100 mg daily and proceed to elective electrical cardioversion in 24 hours
new_options.rationale: Aspirin provides no useful stroke protection peri-cardioversion; therapeutic anticoagulation with heparin, LMWH, DOAC, or warfarin is required (CSANZ/NHFA AF 2018).

new_options.text: Defer cardioversion indefinitely and use rate control with metoprolol without anticoagulation
new_options.rationale: Indefinite deferral of rhythm control loses the symptomatic benefit of restoring sinus rhythm in a first symptomatic episode (EAST-AFNET 4); rate control alone does not exempt from anticoagulation decisions guided by CHA2DS2-VA.

new_options.text: Discharge home, commence flecainide pill-in-pocket strategy and review in clinic next week
new_options.rationale: Pill-in-pocket flecainide is reserved for selected patients with structurally normal hearts, established rhythm-control plan, and proven safety under monitored conditions; it is not appropriate as a first cardioversion attempt in untreated AF with uncertain duration (CSANZ/NHFA AF 2018).

verdicts.new_stem: A 71-year-old woman with CKD stage 3b (eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73m^2) and T2DM (HbA1c 56 mmol/mol) is scheduled tomorrow for an elective coronary angiogram for stable angina. She takes perindopril, atorvastatin, aspirin, metformin and empagliflozin. She is euvolaemic on examination with no peripheral oedema, JVP not raised, clear chest, BP 134/78 mmHg, K 4.1 mmol/L, creatinine 132 micromol/L. She has no decompensated heart failure or current sepsis. The cardiologist plans iso-osmolar low-volume iodinated contrast.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which combined peri-procedural plan most appropriately reduces her risk of contrast-associated AKI and metabolic complications?

new_options.text: Continue all current medications and prescribe IV 0.9 percent sodium chloride 1 mL/kg/h for 6 hours before and 6 hours after contrast
new_options.rationale: Volume expansion is correct, but metformin should be withheld around contrast in advanced CKD (eGFR less than 45) due to lactic acidosis risk, and SGLT2 inhibitors should be paused peri-procedurally given dehydration and euglycaemic DKA risk (KDIGO; AMH; ADS).
source_refs: Australian Diabetes Society peri-procedural SGLT2i guidance

new_options.text: Withhold metformin and empagliflozin on the day of and 48 hours after contrast, give IV 0.9 percent sodium chloride 1 mL/kg/h pre and post, and recheck creatinine before reinstating
new_options.rationale: KDIGO recommends isotonic crystalloid volume expansion in eGFR less than 45 receiving iodinated contrast; metformin should be withheld in eGFR less than 45 around contrast and restarted only after stable renal function; SGLT2 inhibitors are paused peri-procedurally per ADS guidance to reduce euglycaemic DKA risk (KDIGO; AMH; ADS).
source_refs: Australian Diabetes Society peri-procedural SGLT2i guidance

new_options.text: Commence oral N-acetylcysteine 1200 mg twice daily on the day of and after the procedure, continue all usual medications
new_options.rationale: PRESERVE showed no benefit from NAC and KDIGO no longer recommends it; continuing metformin and SGLT2 inhibitor through contrast also misses important medication holds (PRESERVE; KDIGO).

new_options.text: Give IV sodium bicarbonate infusion in place of normal saline, with IV frusemide 40 mg before contrast to maintain urine output
new_options.rationale: PRESERVE showed bicarbonate offered no advantage over saline, and forced diuresis with loop diuretics worsens contrast-associated AKI by causing volume depletion (PRESERVE; KDIGO).

new_options.text: Withhold all antihypertensives for 48 hours before angiogram and recheck creatinine on the day
new_options.rationale: Blanket cessation of antihypertensives is not evidence-based and risks uncontrolled BP; specific medication holds (metformin, SGLT2 inhibitor) are the priority while ACE inhibitor decisions are individualised (KDIGO; AMH).

verdicts.new_stem: A 34-year-old man presents to his GP with two weeks of progressive ankle and periorbital swelling, weight gain of 6 kg, and frothy urine. He has no joint pains, rashes, recent sore throat, skin infection, or haemoptysis. He takes no regular medications. Examination: BP 138/86 mmHg, periorbital and bilateral pitting peripheral oedema to the knees, chest clear, no purpura. Bloods: albumin 22 g/L, cholesterol 8.8 mmol/L, creatinine 88 micromol/L, eGFR over 90, normal FBC and LFTs, complement C3 and C4 normal, ANA and ANCA negative, hepatitis B and C and HIV negative, HbA1c 38 mmol/mol. Urinalysis: protein 4+, blood trace, no leucocytes or nitrites, no red cell casts on microscopy. Spot urine protein-creatinine ratio 480 mg/mmol.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation to establish the underlying diagnosis?

new_options.text: Renal biopsy with light microscopy, immunofluorescence and electron microscopy
new_options.rationale: In an adult with nephrotic-range proteinuria, normal complement, negative serology and excluded secondary causes (HBV, HCV, HIV, diabetes), the underlying glomerulopathy (membranous, FSGS, minimal change) cannot be determined without biopsy. KDIGO recommends renal biopsy in adult nephrotic syndrome where a treatable secondary cause has not been identified.

new_options.text: Serum and urine electrophoresis with free light chain assay to exclude paraprotein
new_options.rationale: Paraprotein workup is reasonable adjunctive testing in older adults or those with hypercalcaemia or anaemia, but in a 34-year-old with classic nephrotic syndrome and a negative initial secondary screen, biopsy will give the diagnosis faster (KDIGO).

new_options.text: Anti-phospholipase A2 receptor (anti-PLA2R) antibody titre as a standalone diagnostic test
new_options.rationale: Anti-PLA2R is highly specific for primary membranous nephropathy but a negative result does not exclude membranous (about 30 percent are seronegative) or other nephrotic glomerulopathies, so it cannot replace biopsy in adults (KDIGO).

new_options.text: Renal tract ultrasound with Doppler to assess kidney size and vascular flow
new_options.rationale: Ultrasound is part of pre-biopsy workup (confirms two kidneys, normal size, excludes hydronephrosis) but does not establish the glomerular diagnosis required to guide immunosuppression (KDIGO).

new_options.text: Empirical trial of high-dose oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily and recheck in 8 weeks
new_options.rationale: Empirical steroid trials are reserved for paediatric minimal change disease; in adults this risks treating the wrong pathology (e.g. membranous, FSGS) and exposes the patient to corticosteroid toxicity without a histological diagnosis (KDIGO).

verdicts.new_stem: A 74-year-old man with CKD stage 4 (eGFR 22 mL/min/1.73m^2) and heart failure attends ED with malaise and palpitations. Medications: irbesartan 300 mg, spironolactone 25 mg, frusemide 40 mg, metoprolol, atorvastatin. He was started on trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 5 days ago for a UTI. Examination: BP 138/82, HR 52 regular, alert. Bloods on arrival: K 7.3 mmol/L, Na 134, Cr 248 micromol/L (was 198 last month), HCO3 19, glucose 6.4. ECG shows sinus bradycardia with prolonged PR, broad QRS approximately 140 ms, and tall peaked T waves in V2-V5. You have given IV calcium gluconate 10 percent 10 mL over 2 minutes and the QRS narrows on repeat ECG.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which combination is the most appropriate next set of interventions?

new_options.text: Repeat IV calcium gluconate 10 percent 10 mL every 30 minutes while awaiting dialysis
new_options.rationale: Calcium stabilises the myocardium but does not lower potassium; repeated calcium without shift or removal therapy leaves the patient at ongoing arrhythmia risk once the calcium wanes (eTG Nephrology).

new_options.text: IV actrapid insulin 10 units with 50 mL of 50 percent dextrose plus nebulised salbutamol 10 mg, stop irbesartan, spironolactone and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, and arrange urgent renal review for dialysis
new_options.rationale: After calcium stabilisation, the priorities are shift therapy (insulin-dextrose plus salbutamol act synergistically), removal of the precipitating drugs (RAAS inhibitor, potassium-sparing diuretic, trimethoprim acts as a potassium-sparing diuretic via ENaC blockade), and arranging dialysis in CKD stage 4 with K 7.3 and ECG changes given temporary nature of shift therapy (eTG Nephrology; UK Renal Association 2023).

new_options.text: Oral sodium polystyrene sulfonate 30 g and IV frusemide 80 mg bolus then admit for telemetry overnight
new_options.rationale: Cation exchangers act over hours, are GI-tolerance limited, and do not protect against arrhythmia in the next 30 minutes; frusemide is of limited utility at eGFR 22 and does not substitute for shift therapy (eTG Nephrology).

new_options.text: IV sodium bicarbonate 100 mmol over 30 minutes plus continue irbesartan and spironolactone at half dose
new_options.rationale: Bicarbonate is only modestly effective in non-acidotic patients and is not first-line shift therapy; continuing RAAS inhibitor and potassium-sparing diuretic in active life-threatening hyperkalaemia is unsafe (eTG Nephrology).

new_options.text: Withhold further interventions and recheck potassium in 4 hours, planning shift therapy only if it remains above 6.5
new_options.rationale: Watchful waiting is unsafe with K 7.3 and persistent precipitants in place; calcium effect lasts only 30 to 60 minutes and rebound arrhythmia is likely (eTG Nephrology).

verdicts.new_stem: A 72-year-old man is referred from his GP after an asymptomatic sodium of 124 mmol/L on a routine pre-operative panel. He has a 50-pack-year smoking history and a chronic cough for three months with mild weight loss. Medications: amlodipine, atorvastatin, sertraline (started six months ago) and carbamazepine (long-standing for trigeminal neuralgia). He denies polydipsia and is otherwise well. Examination: BP 132/78 lying and standing, JVP not raised, mucous membranes moist, no peripheral oedema, normal CVS and respiratory exam. Bloods: Na 124, K 3.9, urea 3.0, creatinine 76 micromol/L, glucose 5.0, serum osmolality 258 mOsm/kg, urine osmolality 412 mOsm/kg, urine sodium 48 mmol/L, TSH 1.8 mIU/L, 9 am cortisol 380 nmol/L.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

new_options.text: Cease sertraline and carbamazepine simultaneously and recheck sodium in 2 weeks
new_options.rationale: Both drugs cause SIADH, but stopping carbamazepine that is controlling trigeminal neuralgia risks an uncontrolled pain flare and ceasing the SSRI abruptly risks discontinuation syndrome; in addition, his smoking history and chronic cough with weight loss must be investigated for paraneoplastic SIADH before assuming drugs are the sole cause (eTG endocrinology).

new_options.text: Arrange CT chest with contrast and review medications with a structured taper plan
new_options.rationale: His euvolaemic hypotonic hyponatraemia with inappropriate urine concentration and natriuresis meets SIADH criteria, but the constellation of 50-pack-year smoking, chronic cough and weight loss mandates imaging for small cell lung cancer as a paraneoplastic cause; medication review with planned taper of contributing drugs is parallel rather than first-step empirical cessation (eTG endocrinology; European 2014).

new_options.text: Commence tolvaptan 15 mg daily and titrate to sodium response over the next week
new_options.rationale: Vaptans are reserved for chronic SIADH refractory to fluid restriction; using them as first-line risks rapid sodium correction with osmotic demyelination, and an underlying cause must be sought first (European 2014).

new_options.text: Empirical hypertonic 3 percent saline 100 mL bolus and admit for ongoing infusion
new_options.rationale: Hypertonic saline is reserved for symptomatic hyponatraemia (seizures, coma, severe confusion); he is asymptomatic and rapid correction in chronic hyponatraemia risks osmotic demyelination (European 2014).

new_options.text: Restrict fluid intake to 800 mL daily and reassure that no further investigation is needed
new_options.rationale: Fluid restriction treats SIADH but missing a small cell lung cancer in a high-risk smoker would be a serious diagnostic error; investigation must accompany treatment (European 2014; eTG endocrinology).

verdicts.new_stem: A 45-year-old woman is referred to endocrine clinic with three months of polyuria and polydipsia (estimated 6 to 7 L per day), worse at night. She has bipolar disorder on lithium 750 mg daily for 12 years, stable, with no recent mood episodes. Medications: lithium, escitalopram. BP 116/72, weight 64 kg, mucous membranes slightly dry. Bloods: Na 148 mmol/L, K 4.2, creatinine 92 micromol/L, eGFR 64, corrected calcium 2.34 mmol/L, glucose 5.2, HbA1c 38, TSH 1.6, lithium 0.7 mmol/L. Plasma osmolality 308 mOsm/kg, urine osmolality 198 mOsm/kg. Water deprivation: urine osmolality 220 mOsm/kg after 6 hours, rising only to 245 mOsm/kg after subcutaneous desmopressin 2 microgram. Her psychiatrist has communicated that lithium remains the preferred mood stabiliser.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate ongoing management strategy?

new_options.text: Discontinue lithium immediately and switch to sodium valproate, then reassess polyuria in 6 weeks
new_options.rationale: Abrupt lithium cessation in stable bipolar disorder risks relapse; lithium nephrogenic DI often persists after cessation due to fixed collecting duct injury; valproate also has TGA black-box warnings and is not an equivalent substitute, especially in women of reproductive age (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic; AMH).

new_options.text: Continue lithium, add amiloride 5 to 10 mg daily, ensure free access to water and monitor sodium and lithium levels
new_options.rationale: Amiloride blocks lithium entry into collecting duct principal cells via ENaC, reducing lithium-induced nephrogenic DI without compromising mood stabilisation; KDIGO and Australian psychiatric guidance support amiloride as the preferred renal-sparing adjunct when lithium is essential, with monitoring of sodium and lithium levels (BMJ Best Practice DI; eTG Endocrinology).

new_options.text: Commence intranasal desmopressin 10 microgram twice daily and continue lithium unchanged
new_options.rationale: She has shown a negligible response to exogenous desmopressin confirming nephrogenic rather than central DI; desmopressin is therefore ineffective and risks fluid retention and hyponatraemia (BMJ Best Practice DI).

new_options.text: Restrict fluid intake to 1.5 L daily and recheck sodium and lithium level in 4 weeks
new_options.rationale: Fluid restriction in nephrogenic DI risks worsening hypernatraemia and acute kidney injury since the obligatory urine output cannot be reduced by reducing intake; lithium toxicity risk rises with dehydration (BMJ Best Practice DI).

new_options.text: Start a high-protein high-sodium diet to enhance free water clearance and continue lithium
new_options.rationale: Treatment of lithium nephrogenic DI uses a low-sodium diet (paradoxically reduces urine output via proximal sodium and water reabsorption) plus thiazide or amiloride, not a high-sodium diet (eTG Endocrinology).

verdicts.new_stem: A 78-year-old woman with CKD stage 4 (eGFR 24 mL/min/1.73m^2) is reviewed after a fall. Medications: perindopril 5 mg, metformin 1 g BD, gabapentin 600 mg three times daily, morphine MR 30 mg twice daily, ciprofloxacin 500 mg BD (day 2 of 5 for uncomplicated UTI started by her GP), and apixaban 5 mg BD for atrial fibrillation. She weighs 58 kg. She is drowsy and ataxic, but rousable, with no focal neurology and no head injury. BP 132/76. Bloods: Cr 220 micromol/L (was 195 last month), eGFR 24, K 4.8, HCO3 21, INR 1.1.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which combination of medication changes is most urgently required?

new_options.text: Reduce gabapentin dose, switch morphine to a less renally cleared opioid such as buprenorphine or fentanyl, and reduce apixaban to 2.5 mg BD given age over 80 and weight under 60 kg
new_options.rationale: Gabapentin and morphine (via active metabolite M6G) are renally cleared and the drowsy ataxic picture is consistent with accumulation; AMH directs dose reduction or substitution for opioids in CKD with buprenorphine or fentanyl preferred. Apixaban dose reduction to 2.5 mg BD is mandated when any two of age over 80, weight under 60 kg, or creatinine over 133 micromol/L apply, and she meets all three.
source_refs: Australian Medicines Handbook 2024 - prescribing in renal impairment
Therapeutic Guidelines: Palliative care - opioids in renal impairment

new_options.text: Stop metformin permanently, continue gabapentin and morphine at current doses, and continue apixaban 5 mg BD
new_options.rationale: Metformin should be reviewed at eGFR under 30 but is not the cause of her drowsy ataxic presentation; missing the opioid and gabapentin accumulation and not dose-reducing apixaban risks ongoing falls and bleeding (AMH).
source_refs: Australian Medicines Handbook 2024 - prescribing in renal impairment

new_options.text: Switch apixaban to warfarin titrated to INR 2.0 to 3.0 and continue all other medications
new_options.rationale: Switching to warfarin in elderly CKD has worse bleeding outcomes than dose-adjusted DOAC and does not address the opioid and gabapentin neurotoxicity driving her symptoms (AMH).
source_refs: Australian Medicines Handbook 2024 - prescribing in renal impairment

new_options.text: Stop ciprofloxacin and switch to oral nitrofurantoin 100 mg BD for the remaining UTI course
new_options.rationale: Nitrofurantoin is contraindicated at eGFR under 45 due to inadequate urinary concentrations and increased systemic toxicity; ciprofloxacin can be dose-reduced if needed but is not the priority change (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; AMH).
source_refs: Australian Medicines Handbook 2024 - prescribing in renal impairment

new_options.text: Stop perindopril today and recheck creatinine and potassium in 1 week
new_options.rationale: A creatinine rise under 30 percent on an ACE inhibitor is acceptable; with potassium 4.8 and BP control, perindopril can continue with monitoring (Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular).

verdicts.new_stem: A 28-year-old woman who arrived from Vietnam 6 months ago is starting a graduate nursing programme at a metropolitan tertiary hospital. She received BCG vaccination in childhood. She is asymptomatic, with no fever, cough, weight loss or night sweats. She has no household TB contacts. She is not immunocompromised, not pregnant, and on no medications. Examination is unremarkable. Pre-employment screening with QuantiFERON-TB Gold returns a positive result. CXR is clear with no parenchymal abnormality, no apical scarring and no calcified granulomata. She has no symptoms or signs of active TB on review. LFTs and HIV serology are normal.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

new_options.text: Commence treatment for latent TB infection with rifampicin 600 mg daily for 4 months after baseline LFTs
new_options.rationale: Positive QuantiFERON with no clinical or radiographic features of active disease confirms latent TB infection. Australian National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee guidance offers 4 months rifampicin or 6 to 9 months isoniazid, with 4 months rifampicin preferred for younger patients due to better completion and lower hepatotoxicity (NTAC; eTG).

new_options.text: Confirm the positive result with a Mantoux tuberculin skin test before treating
new_options.rationale: Repeat testing with Mantoux in a BCG-vaccinated patient adds no diagnostic value; interferon-gamma release assays are more specific in BCG-vaccinated populations and a single positive is sufficient to diagnose latent TB once active disease is excluded (NTAC).

new_options.text: Commence full 4-drug active TB therapy with rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide and ethambutol under directly observed therapy
new_options.rationale: RIPE therapy treats active TB; she has no symptoms and a clear CXR with normal exam, so latent TB monotherapy is the correct intensity, avoiding pyrazinamide hepatotoxicity (NTAC; eTG).

new_options.text: No treatment; arrange annual chest X-ray surveillance and clinical review for 5 years
new_options.rationale: Surveillance without preventive therapy misses the opportunity to reduce her lifetime reactivation risk (around 5 to 10 percent); preventive therapy is recommended in healthcare workers with recent migration from a high-incidence country (NTAC).

new_options.text: Repeat QuantiFERON in 8 weeks; treat only if the second test remains positive
new_options.rationale: Serial testing is used for conversion surveillance, not to confirm a diagnostic positive in someone with clear epidemiological risk; delaying treatment risks her commencing nursing duties without latent TB control (NTAC).

verdicts.new_stem: A 79-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes, COPD and chronic heart failure presents in early August with 36 hours of fever 38.7 degrees C, myalgia, dry cough and progressive breathlessness. She had her annual quadrivalent influenza vaccination 6 weeks ago. Chronic medications include metformin, perindopril, bisoprolol, frusemide and inhaled corticosteroids. On arrival: T 38.7, HR 110, RR 24, BP 134/76, SpO2 92 percent on room air. Bibasal crackles without focal consolidation. CXR clear. Influenza A PCR positive. Bloods: WCC 8.2, CRP 56, eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73m^2, creatinine 134 micromol/L (was 96 last month). She is being admitted for supportive care.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate antiviral and supportive management?

new_options.text: Oral oseltamivir 75 mg once daily (renal-dose reduced) for 5 days, plus targeted supportive care without empirical antibiotics
new_options.rationale: eTG recommends oseltamivir for hospitalised influenza in high-risk patients started as early as possible, even if beyond 48 hours; her eGFR of 38 falls in the 30 to 60 band requiring once-daily dosing per AMH. No focal consolidation, normal WCC and CRP make bacterial superinfection unlikely, so empirical antibiotics are not first-line (eTG Antibiotic; AMH).

new_options.text: Oral oseltamivir 75 mg twice daily standard dose with concurrent IV ceftriaxone for empirical bacterial cover
new_options.rationale: Standard twice-daily dosing is inappropriate at eGFR 38 (accumulation and neuropsychiatric adverse effects); empirical ceftriaxone is unwarranted without focal consolidation, raised WCC, or other evidence of bacterial superinfection (AMH; eTG).

new_options.text: Single oral dose of baloxavir marboxil 40 mg as one-off antiviral therapy
new_options.rationale: Baloxavir is not first-line in Australia for hospitalised influenza; oseltamivir remains the recommended agent in hospitalised high-risk patients (eTG).

new_options.text: No antiviral therapy because symptom onset is approaching 48 hours and she was vaccinated 6 weeks ago
new_options.rationale: eTG explicitly extends oseltamivir use beyond 48 hours in hospitalised high-risk patients; vaccination reduces but does not eliminate severity and does not remove the indication for antivirals once influenza is confirmed (eTG).

new_options.text: IV zanamivir 600 mg twice daily as first-line antiviral therapy in renal impairment
new_options.rationale: IV zanamivir is reserved for severely unwell patients or where oseltamivir resistance is suspected and is not first-line for ward-level hospitalised influenza (eTG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 68-year-old woman presents to her GP with a 6 week history of bilateral shoulder and hip girdle pain and stiffness, worst in the mornings with 90 minutes of stiffness improving through the day. Over the past week she has developed a new left-sided temporal headache that is constant and aching, and yesterday her jaw ached after chewing toast. Her vision is normal and there is no scalp tenderness on direct questioning. She has no fevers, no rash, no joint swelling. Past medical history includes hypertension on perindopril and type 2 diabetes on metformin. Examination: T 36.6, BP 132/78. Shoulder and hip girdle tender, passive range of movement painful but full; temporal arteries palpable and slightly tender on the left; normal fundoscopy, normal visual acuity, no neurological deficit. Bloods: ESR 78 mm/hr, CRP 92 mg/L, CK normal, TSH normal, anti-CCP and rheumatoid factor negative.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial management?

new_options.text: Oral prednisolone 15 mg daily with arrangement of an urgent rheumatology review
new_options.rationale: 15 mg daily is the standard initial dose for uncomplicated PMR, but new temporal headache and jaw claudication indicate likely overlapping giant cell arteritis, which requires substantially higher dose corticosteroid promptly to prevent permanent vision loss (BMJ Best Practice; ACR/EULAR).

new_options.text: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg (60 mg) daily commenced immediately, with urgent temporal artery biopsy arranged within 1 to 2 weeks
new_options.rationale: Clinical features of GCA (new temporal headache, jaw claudication, tender temporal artery on a background of PMR) mandate immediate high-dose prednisolone (1 mg/kg, capped at 60 mg) without waiting for biopsy; biopsy remains useful within 1 to 2 weeks of steroid initiation. Steroid response confirms diagnosis and prevents visual loss (BMJ Best Practice GCA; ARA position statement).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Giant cell arteritis

new_options.text: IV pulse methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days followed by oral prednisolone 60 mg daily
new_options.rationale: IV pulse methylprednisolone is reserved for GCA with visual loss or amaurosis fugax; her vision is intact so oral high-dose prednisolone is the appropriate starting intensity, with IV pulse held in reserve (BMJ Best Practice GCA).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Giant cell arteritis

new_options.text: NSAID monotherapy with naproxen 500 mg twice daily plus PPI cover and review in 2 weeks
new_options.rationale: NSAID monotherapy is inadequate for PMR and would not prevent the catastrophic visual loss of untreated GCA; NSAIDs also worsen renal function and BP in her age group (BMJ Best Practice).

new_options.text: Oral methotrexate 15 mg weekly as initial steroid-sparing monotherapy with folate cover
new_options.rationale: Methotrexate is a steroid-sparing add-on for relapse or difficult tapers; it acts slowly and cannot prevent imminent GCA visual loss; corticosteroid is mandatory upfront (ACR/EULAR).

verdicts.new_stem: A 61-year-old man underwent successful primary PCI with a single drug-eluting stent to the proximal LAD 6 weeks ago for anterior STEMI. He has been compliant with aspirin 100 mg daily, ticagrelor 90 mg twice daily, atorvastatin 80 mg, perindopril 5 mg, and metoprolol 50 mg twice daily. He has no atrial fibrillation, no prior stroke, eGFR 78, and no prior bleeding. He attends cardiology review and asks about antiplatelet duration. Two weeks ago he was diagnosed with a 3 cm right colonic adenocarcinoma on screening colonoscopy and is scheduled for elective laparoscopic right hemicolectomy in 4 weeks. He has had no chest pain since the procedure.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate antiplatelet management plan around the upcoming hemicolectomy?

new_options.text: Continue aspirin perioperatively, stop ticagrelor 5 days before surgery, and restart ticagrelor as soon as surgical haemostasis allows (typically 24 to 72 hours postoperatively)
new_options.rationale: Within the first 6 months after DES PCI for ACS, stent thrombosis risk is highest; CSANZ and NHFA guidance recommend continuing aspirin perioperatively for most non-cardiac surgery and pausing the P2Y12 inhibitor for the shortest possible window (ticagrelor 5 days), then restarting promptly once haemostasis allows. Delaying elective surgery is preferable to lengthening the off-DAPT window when possible (Heart Foundation/CSANZ ACS 2016; eTG).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

new_options.text: Stop both aspirin and ticagrelor 5 days before surgery and resume both after discharge
new_options.rationale: Stopping both antiplatelets within 6 months of DES PCI for ACS substantially raises stent thrombosis risk; aspirin should generally be continued perioperatively if surgical bleeding risk is acceptable (CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

new_options.text: Continue both aspirin and ticagrelor throughout the perioperative period without interruption
new_options.rationale: Continuing both antiplatelets through colorectal surgery markedly raises bleeding risk; the recommended strategy is single antiplatelet (aspirin) during surgery (CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

new_options.text: Bridge ticagrelor with prophylactic low molecular weight heparin from 5 days pre-op until restart of ticagrelor
new_options.rationale: Heparin bridging does not protect against stent thrombosis (a platelet-driven event) and adds bleeding risk; bridging is for atrial fibrillation or mechanical valves on warfarin, not antiplatelets (CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

new_options.text: Proceed with surgery now without changing antiplatelet therapy because the stent was placed more than a month ago
new_options.rationale: Six weeks post-DES for ACS is still within the very high-risk window; the planned management must explicitly address P2Y12 inhibitor timing rather than ignore it (CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

verdicts.new_stem: An 82-year-old woman attends her general practice for review of newly diagnosed non-valvular atrial fibrillation found on a 24 hour Holter for palpitations. Her history includes essential hypertension on perindopril-indapamide, ischaemic stroke 3 years ago with full recovery, and no diabetes or heart failure. She weighs 56 kg. Current medications: perindopril-indapamide 5/1.25 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg, paracetamol as required. BP 132/80, HR 78 irregularly irregular. Bloods today: creatinine 138 micromol/L, eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73m^2, HCO3 24. She has no prior bleeding and her HAS-BLED score is 2. She prefers an oral medication strategy without routine INR monitoring.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate stroke-prevention prescription?

new_options.text: Apixaban 5 mg twice daily as standard non-valvular AF dose
new_options.rationale: Apixaban dose reduction to 2.5 mg twice daily is mandated when any two of age over 80, weight under 60 kg, or creatinine over 133 micromol/L are present; she meets all three, so standard dosing would over-anticoagulate (NHFA/CSANZ; AMH).

new_options.text: Apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily based on age, weight, and creatinine criteria
new_options.rationale: Per AMH and NHFA/CSANZ, apixaban is reduced to 2.5 mg BD when any two of age over 80, weight under 60 kg, or creatinine over 133 micromol/L are met; she fulfils all three, so the reduced dose is required to balance stroke prevention against bleeding (NHFA/CSANZ; AMH).

new_options.text: Warfarin titrated to INR 2.0 to 3.0 with point-of-care monitoring
new_options.rationale: Warfarin remains effective but DOACs are first-line for non-valvular AF in Australia, especially when the patient prefers monitoring-free therapy; warfarin would only be preferred for mechanical valves or moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis (NHFA/CSANZ).

new_options.text: Rivaroxaban 20 mg once daily with the main meal of the day
new_options.rationale: Rivaroxaban requires dose reduction to 15 mg daily at CrCl 15 to 49 mL/min; her eGFR of 38 mandates the reduced dose, so 20 mg is incorrect (AMH).

new_options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily as sole long-term antithrombotic therapy
new_options.rationale: Aspirin is not recommended for stroke prevention in non-valvular AF; it has minimal protective effect against cardioembolic stroke with comparable bleeding risk to anticoagulation (NHFA/CSANZ).

verdicts.new_stem: A 72-year-old man with hypertension and a 30 pack-year smoking history presents to ED after collapsing without warning while shopping. Bystanders report he became briefly stiff with a few arm jerks; he was unresponsive for around 30 seconds and recovered immediately on the floor with no confusion. He had two similar episodes in the past 2 months, one while sitting watching television. Examination: BP 142/82 lying, 140/82 standing, HR 60 sinus, no postural drop, normal cardiovascular and neurological exam. ECG: sinus rhythm 58, PR 220 ms, QRS 110 ms, RBBB plus left anterior fascicular block. Bloods within reference. He drives a private car daily for his work as a courier.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which combination is most appropriate as his next steps?

new_options.text: Admit for telemetry and TTE, advise him not to drive a private vehicle until cause is identified and treated, and notify him of his obligation to inform the licensing authority
new_options.rationale: High-risk cardiac syncope features (age over 60, no prodrome, syncope while sitting, recurrent episodes, bifascicular block) mandate admission with monitoring and echocardiography per ESC 2018. Austroads Assessing Fitness to Drive 2022 imposes a 4-week non-driving period for unexplained syncope at minimum, longer if cardiac, and the driver bears the duty to inform the licensing authority of any condition affecting fitness to drive (ESC; Austroads).
source_refs: ESC 2018 Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Syncope

new_options.text: Admit for telemetry and TTE but allow private driving once telemetry is normal for 48 hours
new_options.rationale: Austroads requires defined non-driving periods after unexplained or cardiac syncope based on aetiology and treatment, not on a brief in-hospital telemetry result; this would breach the standard (Austroads).

new_options.text: Discharge with reassurance, advise hydration and counterpressure manoeuvres for vasovagal syncope, and continue driving
new_options.rationale: His features are atypical for vasovagal (no prodrome, sitting, recurrent, abnormal ECG) and high-risk for cardiac syncope; discharge and continued driving are unsafe (ESC; Austroads).
source_refs: ESC 2018 Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Syncope

new_options.text: Arrange outpatient CT brain and EEG for suspected new-onset seizure disorder and advise no driving for 6 months under epilepsy rules
new_options.rationale: Brief myoclonic jerks during cerebral hypoperfusion (convulsive syncope) are common and do not indicate epilepsy; rapid recovery without confusion argues against seizure, so epilepsy driving rules are misapplied (BMJ Best Practice).

new_options.text: Discharge home for outpatient 24-hour Holter and tilt-table testing within 4 weeks; continue driving in the interim
new_options.rationale: Outpatient deferral and continued driving with high-risk features and bifascicular block exposes him to in-vehicle syncope and third-party harm; admission with monitoring is the standard (ESC; Austroads).
source_refs: ESC 2018 Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Syncope

verdicts.new_stem: A 38-year-old man presents to his GP for a workplace medical. He is asymptomatic, exercises twice a week, drinks 8 standard drinks weekly, has BMI 28, does not smoke, has no diabetes, and no family history of premature CVD. Three separate clinic BP readings over 6 weeks average 156/96, and home BP averaged over 7 days is 152/94. Bloods: total cholesterol 4.6, LDL 2.8, HDL 1.4, triglycerides 1.2, fasting glucose 5.1, eGFR over 90, normal urea/electrolytes and urine ACR. ECG normal. His Australian absolute 5-year CV risk (NVDPA calculator) is 4 percent. He has tried 12 weeks of structured lifestyle change with his GP including reduced salt, DASH-style diet, alcohol reduction to 8 standard drinks per week, and increased exercise; BP today remains 154/94.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Commence perindopril 5 mg daily and continue ongoing lifestyle measures, with home BP review at 6 weeks
new_options.rationale: After an adequate trial of lifestyle change (3 to 6 months) without BP control, NHFA 2016 recommends adding pharmacotherapy in grade 1 to 2 hypertension even at low absolute CV risk. First-line agents are ACE inhibitor, ARB, dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker, or thiazide; perindopril is a reasonable first choice in a younger patient without contraindications (NHFA; eTG).
source_refs: NHFA 2016 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Hypertension in Adults

new_options.text: Continue lifestyle modification for another 6 months before considering pharmacotherapy
new_options.rationale: An adequate 3 to 6 month lifestyle trial has already failed at sustained BP above 140/90; further deferral risks ongoing end-organ exposure without proportional benefit (NHFA 2016).
source_refs: NHFA 2016 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Hypertension in Adults

new_options.text: Refer for 24-hour ambulatory BP monitoring to exclude white-coat hypertension before any pharmacotherapy
new_options.rationale: Clinic and home readings are concordant for sustained hypertension; ABPM is unnecessary when these two agree, and adds delay (NHFA 2016).
source_refs: NHFA 2016 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Hypertension in Adults

new_options.text: Commence atorvastatin 20 mg daily for global CV risk reduction in addition to lifestyle
new_options.rationale: His absolute CV risk is low (4 percent) and lipids are unremarkable; statin is not indicated and does not address his uncontrolled BP (NVDPA; NHFA).
source_refs: NHFA 2016 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Hypertension in Adults

new_options.text: Investigate for secondary hypertension with plasma aldosterone-renin ratio, renal artery imaging and 24-hour urinary metanephrines today
new_options.rationale: Secondary hypertension workup is reserved for resistant hypertension (BP uncontrolled on three agents including a diuretic), young-onset severe hypertension with red flags (hypokalaemia, episodic symptoms), or clinical clues; not first-line at this stage (NHFA 2016).
source_refs: NHFA 2016 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Hypertension in Adults

verdicts.new_stem: A 56-year-old man attends his GP for review of fatigue and nocturia over the last three months. He has hypertension on perindopril 5 mg, BMI 31, and his father has T2DM. He has had two haemoglobinopathy screens incidentally documented in the past (beta thalassaemia minor). He is not on glucocorticoids and has had no transfusions or recent illness. A fasting venous plasma glucose taken in a NATA-accredited laboratory two weeks ago was 7.3 mmol/L; today's laboratory HbA1c is 50 mmol/mol (6.7 percent); a random venous glucose today is 9.4 mmol/L. He has acanthosis nigricans and is otherwise well, with no polyuria, weight loss, or vomiting.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step to establish or refute the diagnosis of type 2 diabetes?

new_options.text: Diagnose type 2 diabetes now on the basis of HbA1c 50 mmol/mol plus fasting glucose 7.3 mmol/L and commence metformin
new_options.rationale: Beta thalassaemia minor alters red cell turnover and can falsely lower or raise HbA1c depending on the assay; Australian Diabetes Society guidance lists haemoglobinopathy as a setting where HbA1c should not be used, requiring confirmation by repeat glycaemic testing (ADS Position; RACGP).

new_options.text: Repeat the fasting venous plasma glucose in a NATA-accredited laboratory on a separate day to confirm fasting glucose at least 7.0 mmol/L
new_options.rationale: Because his haemoglobinopathy invalidates HbA1c, Australian diagnosis must rely on two abnormal glycaemic tests on separate days (fasting glucose at least 7.0, OGTT 2-hour at least 11.1, or random at least 11.1 with classic symptoms); repeating the fasting glucose is the most direct confirmatory test (RACGP; ADS).

new_options.text: Discharge without diagnosis because the random glucose of 9.4 mmol/L is below 11.1 mmol/L
new_options.rationale: An isolated random glucose below 11.1 does not exclude diabetes; his fasting glucose of 7.3 mmol/L already meets the diabetes threshold and requires confirmatory testing, not discharge (RACGP).

new_options.text: Order fructosamine as the primary diagnostic test since HbA1c is unreliable in haemoglobinopathy
new_options.rationale: Fructosamine is not validated as a primary diagnostic test for diabetes in Australian guidelines; confirmation uses standard glycaemic tests (fasting glucose or OGTT) (ADS).

new_options.text: Order an oral glucose tolerance test with 2-hour glucose at 75 g load, but commence metformin today regardless of result
new_options.rationale: Initiating treatment before establishing diagnosis bypasses confirmation; OGTT is an acceptable confirmatory test but treatment must follow the diagnosis (RACGP; ADS).

verdicts.new_stem: A 32-year-old woman attends her GP for preconception counselling. She is planning pregnancy in the next 3 months. Blood tests ordered 8 weeks ago showed TSH 5.6 mIU/L with normal free T4. A repeat panel today shows TSH 5.4 mIU/L, free T4 13 pmol/L (normal), and TPO antibodies markedly elevated at 540 IU/mL. She has no goitre, no compressive symptoms, and no other thyroid history. She has hypercholesterolaemia on atorvastatin and is otherwise well. She has no constipation or weight change.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Commence levothyroxine to target a pre-conception TSH below 2.5 mIU/L and review TFTs in 6 to 8 weeks
new_options.rationale: In women planning pregnancy or in early pregnancy, Australian and international endocrine guidance recommends treating subclinical hypothyroidism (TSH above 2.5 in pregnancy planning, above trimester-specific limits in pregnancy) particularly when TPO antibodies are positive, to reduce miscarriage and pregnancy complications; pre-conception TSH target is below 2.5 mIU/L (ESA position; RACGP Red Book; ATA pregnancy guidelines).

new_options.text: Repeat TFTs and TPO antibodies in 6 to 12 months with no treatment now and advise contraception until reassessed
new_options.rationale: Deferring treatment delays pre-conception optimisation; positive TPO antibodies plus TSH above 2.5 in a woman planning pregnancy is a treatment indication (ESA; ATA).

new_options.text: Stop atorvastatin and recheck thyroid function in 6 weeks since statin may interfere with the TSH assay
new_options.rationale: Statins do not interfere with TSH; atorvastatin should be ceased on confirmation of pregnancy due to fetal risk, but not for thyroid reasons (AMH).

new_options.text: Refer for ultrasound-guided fine needle aspiration of the thyroid for cytology
new_options.rationale: FNA is indicated for suspicious thyroid nodules; she has no nodule (RACGP).

new_options.text: Commence T3 (liothyronine) 20 microgram daily as primary thyroid replacement
new_options.rationale: T3 monotherapy is not recommended for primary hypothyroidism; levothyroxine T4 is the standard (AMH; ESA).

verdicts.new_stem: A 72-year-old woman attends her GP three hours after an episode of left arm and face weakness lasting 35 minutes, now completely resolved. She has hypertension on amlodipine, T2DM on metformin, current smoker (20 pack-years), and no prior atrial fibrillation. Examination is now normal. BP today 168/96, blood glucose 9.2, ECG sinus rhythm, no carotid bruit. She has no anticoagulation, no bleeding tendency, and no recent head injury.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate combination of management steps?

new_options.text: Same-day ED transfer for non-contrast CT brain and CT angiography of head and neck, load with aspirin 300 mg now, and arrange admission to a stroke unit with cardiac monitoring
new_options.rationale: Australian Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines mandate urgent same-day assessment for all suspected TIAs regardless of ABCD2 score, with same-day brain and vessel imaging to identify large-vessel stenosis that may warrant urgent intervention, initial antiplatelet load, and cardiac monitoring to detect occult AF. DAPT for 21 days (aspirin plus clopidogrel) is then recommended after high-risk TIA per CHANCE/POINT data, but the initial step is urgent referral with aspirin loading.

new_options.text: Calculate ABCD2 and refer urgently only if 4 or more; otherwise manage in primary care with aspirin and review in 1 week
new_options.rationale: Australian guidance has moved away from ABCD2-based triage because lower scores still carry meaningful early stroke risk; all suspected TIAs require same-day specialist assessment (Australian Stroke Foundation).

new_options.text: Reassure and arrange outpatient TIA clinic review in 2 weeks with aspirin 100 mg daily today
new_options.rationale: Two-week deferral risks recurrent stroke; same-day specialist assessment with same-day imaging is the standard (Australian Stroke Foundation).

new_options.text: Commence rivaroxaban 20 mg daily empirically for presumed paroxysmal AF and arrange outpatient Holter monitor
new_options.rationale: Empirical anticoagulation without documented AF exposes her to bleeding risk in the immediate post-TIA window before imaging excludes haemorrhage; rhythm monitoring is appropriate but not in place of urgent imaging (Australian Stroke Foundation).

new_options.text: Arrange a carotid Doppler within the next 4 weeks and review her again after the result is back
new_options.rationale: Carotid imaging is critical but must occur within 24 to 48 hours, not weeks; same-day referral with CTA is the standard pathway (Australian Stroke Foundation).

verdicts.new_stem: A 74-year-old woman presents to her GP with a three-week history of new bilateral temporal headache progressively worsening, with scalp tenderness on hair brushing and jaw pain with chewing tough food. Yesterday she had a 10-minute episode of curtain-like vision loss in her right eye that has now resolved. Today on examination her right eye visual acuity is 6/9 (was 6/6 four months ago at optometry) with a relative afferent pupillary defect; left eye 6/6. Temporal arteries tender and pulsatile bilaterally. Bloods: ESR 92 mm/hr, CRP 84 mg/L, Hb 108 g/L, platelets 484. She has hypertension on perindopril and T2DM on metformin (HbA1c 58 mmol/mol). Bone mineral density was T-score -1.4 at the lumbar spine 6 months ago.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?

new_options.text: Admit for IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days followed by oral prednisolone 60 mg daily, with same-day ophthalmology and rheumatology review and arrangement of temporal artery biopsy within 1 to 2 weeks
new_options.rationale: Amaurosis fugax and a new relative afferent pupillary defect with reduced visual acuity indicate ophthalmic involvement of giant cell arteritis, which is a sight-threatening emergency requiring IV pulse methylprednisolone rather than oral prednisolone alone. Biopsy can still be performed within 1 to 2 weeks of steroid initiation. Calcium, vitamin D and PJP and bone protection planning are needed but not immediate (eTG Rheumatology; ARA position).

new_options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 60 mg daily today and arrange temporal artery biopsy within 1 to 2 weeks
new_options.rationale: Oral prednisolone 60 mg is standard for GCA without visual involvement; the presence of amaurosis fugax and RAPD escalates to IV pulse methylprednisolone (eTG; ARA).

new_options.text: Order CT brain today and start prednisolone only if temporal artery biopsy is positive within 2 weeks
new_options.rationale: CT brain does not diagnose GCA and waiting for biopsy risks permanent blindness within hours to days; steroid must be started on clinical suspicion (eTG).

new_options.text: Commence tocilizumab subcutaneous 162 mg weekly as first-line monotherapy
new_options.rationale: Tocilizumab is steroid-sparing add-on therapy for established disease; it does not replace acute high-dose corticosteroid in the emergency setting (eTG; ARA).

new_options.text: Treat empirically as migraine with sumatriptan 50 mg and arrange neurology follow-up in 2 weeks
new_options.rationale: New severe headache in someone over 50 with raised inflammatory markers, jaw claudication and visual loss is not migraine, and triptans are contraindicated in suspected ischaemic disease (eTG Neurology).

verdicts.new_stem: A 48-year-old previously well woman presents to ED 14 hours after the abrupt onset of the worst headache of her life while gardening. The headache reached maximum intensity within seconds and was associated with one episode of vomiting. She now has no neurological deficits, GCS 15, no neck stiffness, no rash, no photophobia. BP 162/94. She is not anticoagulated and has no recent trauma. A non-contrast CT brain is performed and reported by an experienced radiologist as normal. She has had similar but less severe headaches with exertion previously, never investigated.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Perform lumbar puncture for CSF xanthochromia and red cell count
new_options.rationale: Non-contrast CT brain has near-100 percent sensitivity for SAH within 6 hours of onset but sensitivity falls beyond that window; her presentation 14 hours after onset means a normal CT does not exclude SAH, and lumbar puncture for xanthochromia (visible from 12 hours after bleed) is the next step. CT angiography is added if LP confirms SAH (eTG Neurology; ACEM).

new_options.text: Discharge with simple analgesia since the CT brain is normal and arrange neurology follow-up in 1 week
new_options.rationale: Discharge after a normal CT brain done beyond 6 hours from onset for thunderclap headache is unsafe; CT sensitivity drops below 100 percent and SAH must still be excluded by LP (eTG).

new_options.text: Proceed directly to CT angiography of intracranial vessels for unruptured aneurysm screening
new_options.rationale: CTA can miss a recently bled small aneurysm that has thrombosed, and identifies incidental aneurysms in around 3 percent of adults whose findings cannot be linked to the headache without confirming a bleed; LP is the correct next step (eTG; ACEM).

new_options.text: Empirical IV ceftriaxone 2 g and IV dexamethasone 10 mg for presumed bacterial meningitis
new_options.rationale: No fever, neck stiffness, photophobia or rash; meningitis cover is not the priority and the headache pattern fits thunderclap rather than meningitic onset (eTG Antibiotic).

new_options.text: Order MRI brain with gadolinium and FLAIR sequences as the definitive next investigation
new_options.rationale: MRI with FLAIR or GRE can demonstrate subacute SAH but is less universally available and slower than LP; in Australian practice, LP is the recommended next step after a non-diagnostic CT beyond 6 hours (eTG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 28-year-old woman is brought to ED 25 minutes after eating a peanut-containing dessert. She has a known peanut allergy. On arrival: stridor and dyspnoea, widespread urticaria and lip swelling, BP 78/42, HR 132, SpO2 91 percent on 15 L non-rebreather, RR 28, alert but distressed. You give IM adrenaline 0.5 mg into the anterolateral mid-thigh and lie her supine with legs elevated. Five minutes later, BP is 82/46, HR 130, RR 28, SpO2 92 percent, with persistent stridor. A second IM adrenaline 0.5 mg is given. Five minutes after that, BP is 84/48, HR 128, SpO2 92 percent, stridor persists. Two large-bore IVs are in. She has no past cardiac history and takes no regular medications.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Commence IV adrenaline infusion at 5 microgram per minute titrated to clinical response, give 20 mL/kg IV crystalloid bolus, and prepare for advanced airway management
new_options.rationale: After two doses of IM adrenaline without adequate response, ASCIA recommends IV adrenaline infusion (5 to 15 microgram per minute titrated) alongside aggressive fluid resuscitation; persistent stridor and shock require airway preparation including senior anaesthetic and ENT support, with surgical airway equipment ready (ASCIA 2024; ANZCOR).

new_options.text: Continue IM adrenaline 0.5 mg every 5 minutes indefinitely and avoid intravenous adrenaline
new_options.rationale: Refractory anaphylaxis (no response after two IM doses) is the indication for IV adrenaline infusion under monitored conditions; persisting with IM alone delays effective therapy (ASCIA 2024).

new_options.text: Give IV hydrocortisone 200 mg and IV promethazine 25 mg and observe for further response
new_options.rationale: Steroids and antihistamines are adjuncts only and do not reverse acute anaphylaxis; promethazine can worsen hypotension; neither addresses refractory disease (ASCIA 2024; AMH).

new_options.text: Give IV glucagon 1 to 5 mg bolus as the next escalation step
new_options.rationale: Glucagon is reserved for refractory anaphylaxis in patients on beta-blocker; she is on no medications, so IV adrenaline infusion is the next step (ASCIA 2024).

new_options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 5 mg and nebulised adrenaline 5 mg via oxygen-driven nebuliser, then reassess
new_options.rationale: Nebulised therapies are adjuncts for persistent bronchospasm or laryngeal oedema after systemic adrenaline; they do not replace IV adrenaline infusion in refractory shock (ASCIA 2024).

verdicts.new_stem: A 58-year-old woman with well-controlled SLE on hydroxychloroquine presents with 10 days of fatigue, exertional dyspnoea and dark urine, with mild scleral icterus for 3 days. She has no fever, weight loss or recent infections, has not started any new medications, and has never been transfused. Examination: pallor, scleral icterus, 2 cm spleen tip, no lymphadenopathy, no acrocyanosis. Bloods: Hb 62 g/L, MCV 102, reticulocytes 11 percent, LDH 920 U/L, haptoglobin under 0.1, unconjugated bilirubin 64 micromol/L, DAT IgG strongly positive and C3d weakly positive, blood film spherocytes and polychromasia, cold agglutinin titre negative. She is haemodynamically stable but has new exertional angina on transferring to the bed. ECG shows no ischaemic changes; troponin is mildly elevated (28 ng/L, normal under 14).
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which combination of treatments is most appropriate as first-line management?

new_options.text: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily, transfuse the least incompatible packed red cells with close clinical and laboratory monitoring, and add folate replacement
new_options.rationale: Warm AIHA first-line therapy is prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day. Her Hb of 62 with exertional angina and troponin rise reflects symptomatic anaemia threatening end-organ perfusion, which is an indication to transfuse despite cross-match difficulty; least incompatible units are given with close monitoring. Folate supports the marrow response (BSH AIHA 2017; eTG Haematology; ANZSBT).
source_refs: BSH Guidelines on AIHA in Adults 2017
ANZSBT Guidelines for the Administration of Blood Products

new_options.text: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily and strictly avoid blood transfusion regardless of severity
new_options.rationale: Transfusion is not absolutely contraindicated in warm AIHA; least incompatible units may be life-saving when there is end-organ ischaemia (here, exertional angina with troponin rise) (BSH AIHA; ANZSBT).
source_refs: BSH Guidelines on AIHA in Adults 2017
ANZSBT Guidelines for the Administration of Blood Products

new_options.text: Urgent splenectomy and IV immunoglobulin 1 g/kg as first-line therapy
new_options.rationale: Splenectomy and IVIg are not first-line in warm AIHA; prednisolone is. Splenectomy is second-line for steroid-refractory or relapsing disease, after vaccination against encapsulated organisms (BSH AIHA).
source_refs: BSH Guidelines on AIHA in Adults 2017

new_options.text: Plasma exchange with fresh frozen plasma replacement and rituximab 375 mg/m^2 weekly
new_options.rationale: Plasma exchange is used in TTP and severe cold agglutinin disease with hyperviscosity, not warm AIHA. Rituximab is second-line therapy for steroid-refractory cases (BSH AIHA).
source_refs: BSH Guidelines on AIHA in Adults 2017

new_options.text: Continue hydroxychloroquine only and recheck FBC in 1 week before considering steroid initiation
new_options.rationale: Symptomatic warm AIHA with Hb 62 and cardiac compromise is not safely managed expectantly; immediate prednisolone is required (BSH AIHA; eTG Haematology).
source_refs: BSH Guidelines on AIHA in Adults 2017

verdicts.new_stem: A 74-year-old woman attends ED with one day of right-sided pleuritic chest pain and mild exertional dyspnoea. She had a knee arthroplasty 8 weeks ago. She has hypertension on perindopril, osteoarthritis, no haemoptysis, no calf swelling, no malignancy and is otherwise active. Examination: comfortable at rest, clear chest, no calf signs, SpO2 96 percent on room air. ECG sinus tachycardia 102. CXR normal. Bloods: troponin negative, FBC normal, eGFR 68. Wells score is 1.5 (recent surgery 1.5; PE most likely diagnosis criterion not counted). Age-adjusted D-dimer returns at 680 microgram/L FEU.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Exclude PE based on Wells unlikely plus D-dimer 680 microgram/L below the age-adjusted cut-off of 740, and discharge with safety netting
new_options.rationale: In Wells PE-unlikely patients over 50, the age-adjusted D-dimer cut-off is age x 10 microgram/L FEU (740 here). A value of 680 lies below this cut-off and safely excludes PE per ADJUST-PE and eTG, sparing her CTPA contrast and radiation; safety netting and follow-up are appropriate.

new_options.text: Proceed to CTPA because the D-dimer is above the standard fixed cut-off of 500 microgram/L
new_options.rationale: Applying the fixed cut-off in patients over 50 over-investigates them; age-adjusted cut-off is the correct rule and avoids unnecessary CTPA (ADJUST-PE; eTG).

new_options.text: Start therapeutic anticoagulation empirically and image with CTPA within 24 hours
new_options.rationale: Empirical anticoagulation pending imaging is reserved for Wells-likely patients with delayed imaging, not Wells-unlikely with a negative age-adjusted D-dimer (eTG; BTS).

new_options.text: Perform V/Q scan as the first-line confirmatory test because CXR is normal
new_options.rationale: V/Q is preferred when contrast is contraindicated; in her case PE is already excluded by Wells-unlikely plus negative age-adjusted D-dimer (eTG).

new_options.text: Repeat D-dimer in 24 hours before deciding on imaging
new_options.rationale: Repeating D-dimer adds no diagnostic information when the original is already below the age-adjusted cut-off; PE is excluded (eTG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 72-year-old man is on day 8 post coronary artery bypass grafting. He has been on prophylactic enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily since day 1 plus an intraoperative and 48-hour post-op unfractionated heparin infusion. He develops a new right calf DVT confirmed on ultrasound. Platelets have fallen from 280 to 78 (a 72 percent drop). No bleeding, no skin necrosis, no schistocytes on film. PF4-heparin ELISA is strongly positive and a serotonin release assay is pending. 4Ts score is 7. He has CKD with eGFR 38 (creatinine 168 micromol/L) and severe hepatic dysfunction from chronic right heart failure (bilirubin 62, INR 1.6).
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate anticoagulation strategy?

new_options.text: Stop all heparin (UFH and LMWH) and commence IV bivalirudin at adjusted dose given combined renal and hepatic impairment, with close aPTT monitoring
new_options.rationale: HIT requires stopping all heparins and starting a non-heparin therapeutic anticoagulant. Argatroban is preferred in renal impairment but contraindicated in significant hepatic dysfunction (predominantly hepatic clearance), and fondaparinux is renally cleared and contraindicated at eGFR under 30. Bivalirudin has both renal and hepatic dose-adjustable clearance, making it the appropriate choice when both organs are impaired (ASH 2018; eTG).

new_options.text: Stop heparin and commence fondaparinux 7.5 mg SC daily off-label at standard dose
new_options.rationale: Fondaparinux is renally cleared and contraindicated at eGFR under 30; her eGFR of 38 sits in the dose-reduction zone but combined with hepatic dysfunction makes bivalirudin a safer choice (ASH 2018; AMH).

new_options.text: Stop heparin and commence IV argatroban at standard dose
new_options.rationale: Argatroban is hepatically cleared and contraindicated in significant hepatic dysfunction (bilirubin 62, INR 1.6); it would accumulate and over-anticoagulate (ASH 2018; AMH).

new_options.text: Stop heparin and immediately commence warfarin bridging to therapeutic INR 2.0 to 3.0
new_options.rationale: Warfarin must not be started in acute HIT until platelets recover above 150, due to risk of warfarin-induced skin necrosis from protein C depletion (ASH 2018).

new_options.text: Stop all heparin and observe expectantly without alternative anticoagulation until platelets recover
new_options.rationale: HIT is prothrombotic; stopping heparin without substitute therapeutic anticoagulation leaves him at high risk of further venous and arterial events (ASH 2018).

verdicts.new_stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with one week of mucocutaneous bruising, gum bleeding, two short epistaxis episodes, and today new heavy menstrual bleeding requiring an overnight pad change every 90 minutes. She takes no medications, has not been recently vaccinated, has no recent viral illness, no pregnancy. No lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly. Bloods: isolated platelets 4 x10^9/L with normal Hb, WCC, differential, coagulation profile, renal and liver function. Blood film: large platelets, no schistocytes, no blasts. HIV, hepatitis B and C, H. pylori, ANA all negative. She has no contraindication to corticosteroids or IVIG. The team plans urgent treatment.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment?

new_options.text: IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days plus IVIG 1 g/kg as combination first-line therapy
new_options.rationale: Severe ITP (platelets under 20 with active bleeding requiring intervention) is treated with combination corticosteroid plus IVIG for rapid platelet rise, per ASH 2019 and eTG; IVIG raises platelets within 24 to 48 hours, while corticosteroid (oral or pulsed IV) provides sustained immunomodulation. Heavy menstrual bleeding with platelets of 4 is bleeding sufficient to warrant combination, not monotherapy (ASH 2019; eTG Haematology).

new_options.text: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily as monotherapy and review platelet count in 1 week
new_options.rationale: Corticosteroid monotherapy takes days to weeks to raise platelets; with active heavy bleeding and platelets of 4, the more rapid combined IVIG plus steroid response is preferred (ASH 2019).

new_options.text: Platelet transfusion of 2 adult therapeutic doses to correct count above 50 x10^9/L
new_options.rationale: Transfused platelets are rapidly consumed by the same autoantibody and rarely raise count meaningfully in stable ITP; transfusion is reserved for life-threatening haemorrhage (intracranial, major GI) alongside other therapies (ASH 2019; ANZSBT).
source_refs: ANZSBT Guidelines for the Administration of Blood Products

new_options.text: Rituximab 375 mg/m^2 weekly for 4 weeks as first-line therapy
new_options.rationale: Rituximab is second-line for steroid-refractory or relapsing ITP, not first-line (ASH 2019).

new_options.text: Bone marrow biopsy first to confirm the diagnosis before commencing any treatment
new_options.rationale: Bone marrow biopsy is not required in typical adult ITP with isolated thrombocytopenia and normal film; treatment must not be delayed when bleeding is active (ASH 2019).

verdicts.new_stem: A 48-year-old man is referred from his GP after a routine FBC for life insurance showed WCC 86 x10^9/L with a full myeloid maturation spectrum (myelocytes, metamyelocytes, basophilia 5 percent, eosinophilia 4 percent, blasts under 2 percent), Hb 124, platelets 480. He has mild left upper quadrant discomfort, no fevers, weight loss, or B symptoms. He has 6 cm splenomegaly. He has a 12-year history of bipolar disorder on lithium 800 mg daily and has a recurrent left-sided pleural effusion from chronic congestive heart failure (LVEF 40 percent). Cytogenetics confirm t(9;22) and BCR-ABL1 transcript by PCR. Bone marrow shows 1 percent blasts (chronic phase). He has no other comorbidities.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line BCR-ABL1 tyrosine kinase inhibitor?

new_options.text: Imatinib 400 mg orally daily
new_options.rationale: Chronic phase CML is treated with a BCR-ABL1 TKI. Among second-generation TKIs, dasatinib is associated with pleural effusion and is contraindicated in his recurrent CHF-related effusion; nilotinib has cardiac and QT risks; ponatinib has significant vascular toxicity. Imatinib remains a reasonable first-line in older patients or those with comorbidities (pleural effusion, cardiac disease) where the side-effect profile favours it over second-generation TKIs (eviQ CML; HSANZ).

new_options.text: Dasatinib 100 mg orally daily
new_options.rationale: Dasatinib is associated with pleural effusion in 20 to 30 percent of patients; contraindicated in someone with a recurrent CHF-related pleural effusion (eviQ; AMH).

new_options.text: Nilotinib 300 mg orally twice daily
new_options.rationale: Nilotinib has cardiac and QT-prolongation risks and significant interactions with QT-prolonging drugs; lithium also prolongs QT, making this combination cardiotoxic (eviQ; AMH).

new_options.text: Ponatinib 45 mg orally daily as first-line therapy
new_options.rationale: Ponatinib is reserved for T315I mutation or TKI-resistant disease due to significant vascular and arterial thrombotic toxicity; not first-line in chronic phase (eviQ).

new_options.text: Hydroxyurea 1 to 2 g daily as definitive cytoreductive therapy
new_options.rationale: Hydroxyurea controls counts but does not target BCR-ABL1 biology; used as a bridge before TKI in severe hyperleucocytosis, not as definitive therapy (eviQ).

verdicts.new_stem: A 24-year-old previously well woman has been diagnosed with classical Hodgkin lymphoma after excisional left supraclavicular lymph node biopsy. Immunohistochemistry: CD30 positive, CD15 positive, CD20 negative Reed-Sternberg cells in a mixed inflammatory infiltrate. She reports drenching night sweats, fevers above 38, and 8 kg weight loss over 6 weeks. PET-CT shows supradiaphragmatic-only nodal involvement (left supraclavicular, mediastinal, bilateral cervical). Bloods: Hb 116, WCC normal, ESR 68, LDH normal, HIV negative. Echocardiogram shows LVEF 60 percent. Pulmonary function tests are normal. She has not commenced any treatment yet. She has no children but wishes to preserve fertility.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step before commencing chemotherapy?

new_options.text: Refer urgently for fertility preservation counselling and oocyte or embryo cryopreservation, then commence ABVD chemotherapy
new_options.rationale: ABVD chemotherapy (standard first-line for early-stage classical Hodgkin lymphoma with B symptoms is generally early-unfavourable disease per Australian and European stratification) carries low to moderate gonadotoxicity but the cumulative effect with potential radiotherapy and BEACOPP for relapse means fertility preservation should be offered before treatment begins. eviQ and HSANZ explicitly recommend urgent fertility referral before chemotherapy in young patients (eviQ Hodgkin; HSANZ).

new_options.text: Commence ABVD chemotherapy immediately and defer fertility discussion until after first cycle
new_options.rationale: Delaying fertility discussion until after chemotherapy starts misses the window for oocyte or embryo cryopreservation (which requires ovarian stimulation pre-treatment); fertility referral must occur before first cycle (eviQ).

new_options.text: Commence BEACOPP chemotherapy as first-line for B symptoms instead of ABVD
new_options.rationale: ABVD is standard first-line in Australia for classical Hodgkin lymphoma; BEACOPP is reserved for advanced-stage disease or relapse, with greater gonadotoxicity (eviQ; HSANZ).

new_options.text: Proceed directly to involved-site radiotherapy as monotherapy without chemotherapy
new_options.rationale: Combined modality (chemotherapy plus radiotherapy in selected cases) is standard; radiotherapy monotherapy is not used for HL with B symptoms (eviQ).

new_options.text: Repeat excisional lymph node biopsy to confirm the immunohistochemistry findings before treatment
new_options.rationale: Diagnosis is already established with CD30 positive CD15 positive CD20 negative Reed-Sternberg cells; repeat biopsy is not required and delays treatment (eviQ).

verdicts.new_stem: A 70-year-old man is being evaluated for symptomatic multiple myeloma. He has back pain (mid-thoracic, worse at night), 4 kg unintentional weight loss, two recent pneumonias, T6 spinal tenderness without neurological deficit. Bloods: Hb 92 g/L, creatinine 198 micromol/L (eGFR 28; baseline 78), corrected calcium 2.78 mmol/L (RR 2.10 to 2.60), albumin 28, IgG kappa paraprotein 42 g/L, kappa free light chain 320 mg/L (ratio 28), urinary Bence Jones protein positive. CT shows multiple lytic lesions and a T6 compression fracture. Bone marrow biopsy: 45 percent clonal plasma cells.
