verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate combination of next steps in management?

new_options.text: Urgent MRI whole spine for cord compression risk, IV hydration with isotonic crystalloid, IV zoledronic acid (dose-adjusted for renal function), and refer to haematology to commence bortezomib-based induction
new_options.rationale: He meets CRAB criteria with active myeloma. The T6 compression fracture in painful spine mandates urgent MRI to exclude cord compression. Hypercalcaemia requires IV hydration and bisphosphonate (zoledronic acid is the standard, with dose adjustment at eGFR 28 per AMH); bortezomib-based induction (e.g. VTd or VRd) is preferred over lenalidomide-based regimens in significant renal impairment (eviQ Myeloma; HSANZ; eTG).
source_refs: HSANZ position statement on multiple myeloma

new_options.text: Refer for bone marrow biopsy and skeletal survey before commencing any treatment
new_options.rationale: Bone marrow (45 percent plasma cells) and CT skeletal imaging are already done; further delay risks cord compression and worsening renal failure (eviQ).

new_options.text: Commence high-dose dexamethasone 40 mg daily and discharge on outpatient follow-up
new_options.rationale: Dexamethasone alone is inadequate induction and discharge with painful compression fracture and severe AKI is unsafe; multidisciplinary inpatient management is required (eviQ).

new_options.text: Commence high-dose lenalidomide-based induction immediately without renal dose adjustment
new_options.rationale: Lenalidomide requires substantial dose reduction at eGFR under 30 and bortezomib-based regimens are preferred in significant renal impairment due to faster reduction of paraprotein and better renal recovery (eviQ; AMH).

new_options.text: Commence emergency haemodialysis to clear paraprotein and defer chemotherapy until renal function improves
new_options.rationale: Dialysis does not effectively clear intact paraprotein; renal recovery in myeloma is driven by reducing light chain production via systemic therapy (bortezomib-based), with dialysis reserved for life-threatening uraemia or fluid overload (eviQ).

verdicts.new_stem: A 56-year-old man with cirrhosis presents with massive haematemesis. He arrives in haemorrhagic shock (BP 78/42, HR 142, GCS 13). The massive transfusion protocol is activated and he receives 6 units packed red cells, 4 units FFP and 1 pool of platelets via rapid infuser over 60 minutes. He is being continued on the MTP and IV tranexamic acid 1 g over 10 minutes was given on arrival. Repeat bloods now: Hb 78, platelets 62, INR 2.1, fibrinogen 1.1 g/L, potassium 6.2 mmol/L, ionised calcium 0.84 mmol/L (RR 1.10 to 1.30), pH 7.18, lactate 8.4, base excess minus 12, magnesium 0.62, temperature 35.4 degrees C. ECG shows tall T waves in V2 to V4 with PR 200 ms; QRS 100 ms.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate single next intervention?

new_options.text: IV calcium gluconate 10 percent 10 mL given over 2 to 3 minutes via a peripheral large-bore cannula
new_options.rationale: Severe ionised hypocalcaemia (0.84) from MTP is immediately life-threatening because it depresses myocardial contractility, prolongs coagulation, and amplifies hyperkalaemia toxicity; ANZSBT MTP guidance recommends calcium replacement at every 4-unit interval and when ionised calcium falls below 1.1. Calcium also stabilises the myocardium against the concomitant hyperkalaemia, addressing two life-threats simultaneously (ANZSBT MTP; NBA PBM).

new_options.text: IV insulin 10 units with 50 mL of 50 percent dextrose to shift potassium intracellularly
new_options.rationale: Insulin-dextrose is reasonable for hyperkalaemia, but with ionised calcium 0.84 and ongoing MTP the calcium deficit is the more immediate cardiac threat and calcium replacement also stabilises the membrane against the hyperkalaemia; insulin without calcium first does not address the broader hypocalcaemia (ANZSBT MTP).

new_options.text: IV sodium bicarbonate 100 mmol bolus to correct metabolic acidosis
new_options.rationale: Bicarbonate does not correct lactic acidosis from hypoperfusion and acutely worsens ionised hypocalcaemia by increasing albumin-bound calcium fraction (ANZSBT MTP).

new_options.text: Active rewarming to address hypothermia as the priority complication
new_options.rationale: Hypothermia worsens the coagulopathy of the lethal triad and rewarming is essential, but it is parallel work; ionised calcium 0.84 with hyperkalaemia and ECG changes is the more immediate single life-threat (ANZSBT MTP).

new_options.text: Stop the massive transfusion protocol and reassess once haemodynamics improve
new_options.rationale: He remains in haemorrhagic shock with ongoing variceal bleeding; pausing MTP risks exsanguination. Metabolic complications are addressed alongside continued resuscitation, not by stopping it (ANZSBT MTP).
